title: Costa Rica Tarrazu: Honey, Citrus, and a Clean Finish for Single-Origin Espresso
hero_image_alt: A clear glass espresso cup on a white marble surface showing a golden-amber espresso with a thin, uniform crema, a drizzle of honey on a small plate and a slice of orange arranged beside the cup, lush green coffee plants visible through a window, bright clean natural light filling the scene
intro: Tarrazu is Costa Rica's crown jewel and one of the cleanest, sweetest single-origin espressos you can pull. Grown at 1,200 to 1,900 metres in the mountains south of San Jose, Tarrazu coffee is defined by crystalline clarity, honey sweetness, citrus brightness, and a finish so clean it seems to disappear the moment you swallow — leaving only the memory of sweetness on your palate. If you have been wanting to try single-origin espresso but find some origins too intense or unusual, Tarrazu is the perfect entry point: familiar enough to feel comfortable, yet complex enough to reveal why single origins are worth the exploration.

body.heading: Tarrazu's Terroir and Quality Culture
body.content: The Tarrazu region — formally the canton of Dota and the surrounding areas of Leon Cortes and Tarrazu in the Los Santos zone — benefits from a combination of altitude, soil, and climate that consistently produces some of the highest-scoring coffees in Central America. The mountains here are steep, with farms clinging to slopes at angles that make mechanized harvesting impossible, ensuring every cherry is hand-picked at peak ripeness. The soil is volcanic, well-drained, and rich in organic matter from the cloud forest that blankets the higher elevations. The dry season from December to March provides ideal conditions for drying coffee on raised beds and patios. But terroir alone does not explain Tarrazu's quality. Costa Rica has one of the most advanced and quality-focused coffee cultures in the producing world. The country was the first to ban the cultivation of Robusta, ensuring that only Arabica is grown. Farmers have access to sophisticated processing equipment, agricultural extension services, and a domestic market that values quality. Many Tarrazu farms are operated by families who have been growing coffee for three or more generations and who have continuously invested in better varieties, better processing, and better drying infrastructure. The dominant varieties are Caturra, Catuai, and Villa Sarchi, with increasing plantings of SL-28, Gesha, and other high-quality varieties in competition-focused lots. Most Tarrazu coffee is washed — fully washed with clean water fermentation and mechanical demucilaging — which produces the origin's trademark clarity. Honey processing, where some or all of the mucilage is left on the bean during drying, has become a specialty focus in Tarrazu, and honey-processed lots from this region are among the best in the world, adding a layer of stone fruit sweetness and body to the naturally clean profile.

body.heading: Flavour Profile: Sweet, Bright, and Impeccably Clean
body.content: The first thing you notice about a well-pulled Tarrazu espresso is the sweetness. It arrives immediately and unambiguously — honey, golden syrup, ripe peach — with a clarity that makes you wonder if someone added sweetener to the cup. This sweetness is Tarrazu's hallmark, and it comes from the slow cherry maturation at altitude and the meticulous processing that preserves every sugar molecule the plant produced. The acidity is bright but refined: orange, mandarin, and lemon zest, with a juiciness that reads as fruit rather than sourness. Unlike the aggressive phosphoric acid of Kenyan coffee, Tarrazu's citric and malic acids are gentle and well-integrated, providing lift and vibrancy without dominating the cup. The body is medium — lighter than Brazilian or Sumatran but with enough substance to feel satisfying in a small espresso cup. The mouthfeel is silky and clean, without any of the grittiness or heaviness that can characterise less carefully processed coffees. But Tarrazu's defining characteristic is its finish. The flavours do not linger and evolve the way Kenyan or Ethiopian espresso does. Instead, they resolve cleanly and quickly, leaving a sweet, pleasant memory but no residue or aftertaste. This clean finish is what makes Tarrazu such an addictive single-origin espresso — it invites you to take another sip immediately, to experience that wave of sweetness and citrus one more time. Honey-processed Tarrazu adds stone fruit complexity (apricot, nectarine) and a slightly fuller body while preserving the clean finish. Black honey, where nearly all the mucilage remains, produces the most complex and full-bodied Tarrazu lots — closer in weight to a Colombian but retaining that unmistakable Costa Rican clarity and clean exit.

body.heading: Brewing Tarrazu as Single-Origin Espresso
body.content: Tarrazu coffee is one of the most rewarding origins to brew as single-origin espresso because it naturally possesses the balance that many origins struggle to achieve under the intensity of espresso extraction. The sweetness is high enough to counterbalance the concentrated acidity, and the clean finish means there is no risk of the muddiness or bitterness that can plague less refined coffees at espresso concentration. On any Arco grinder, set the grind to a medium-fine espresso setting — similar to what you would use for Colombian Huila. Tarrazu beans are moderately dense, well-sorted, and very uniform in size, which means they grind consistently and produce even particle distributions with minimal fines. This uniformity makes Tarrazu pucks exceptionally easy to extract evenly — one of the reasons the origin is so forgiving. Aim for a 1:2 to 1:2.3 ratio — eighteen grams in, thirty-six to forty-one grams out — over twenty-five to thirty seconds. The standard 1:2 ratio produces a sweeter, more concentrated shot that emphasises the honey and body. The longer 1:2.3 ratio opens up the citrus brightness and gives the finish an even cleaner, more transparent quality. Both are excellent — experiment and find your preference. For temperature, set the Arco Doppio or Studio PID to ninety-three degrees Celsius. This moderate temperature extracts the full sweetness without pushing the citric acids into sharpness. The Arco Primo handles Tarrazu beautifully at its default temperature range — no special preparation needed. Pre-infusion of two to three seconds helps, but Tarrazu is forgiving enough that a standard nine-bar extraction also produces clean, even shots. If you are new to single-origin espresso, Tarrazu is the origin to start with on your Arco setup. Pull three or four shots, adjusting grind by one step between each, and taste the progression from slightly under-extracted (more citrus, less sweetness) to slightly over-extracted (less brightness, more body). Tarrazu makes these differences legible and educational without ever producing an unpleasant cup.

body.heading: Serving Styles and Seasonal Enjoyment
body.content: Tarrazu espresso is exceptional both black and with milk, though the preparations that showcase it best depend on the processing method. Washed Tarrazu is at its finest served black — a straight espresso or a lungo that lets the crystalline sweetness and citrus shine without any interference. The clean finish makes it an outstanding espresso to drink alongside water, alternating sips to cleanse the palate and renew the experience of that honey sweetness. A cortado with washed Tarrazu adds a lovely creaminess while preserving the citrus brightness — a beautiful mid-morning drink. Honey-processed Tarrazu, with its fuller body and stone fruit sweetness, stands up well in a flat white or small cappuccino. The apricot and nectarine notes provide enough weight to cut through milk, and the natural sweetness means you will not miss sugar. For iced preparations, Tarrazu is outstanding. The high sweetness and clean finish translate perfectly to cold service, where the absence of lingering bitterness that plagues most iced espresso makes Tarrazu taste almost like a cold-pressed juice — bright, sweet, refreshing. Pull a double shot over ice on a warm afternoon and you will understand why single-origin iced espresso is worth the effort. Tarrazu is also a seasonal pleasure to look for. The Costa Rican harvest runs from November to March, and freshly roasted new-crop Tarrazu typically arrives at specialty roasters between March and June. Buying fresh-crop Tarrazu and enjoying it within its peak freshness window — two to six weeks after roast — is one of the highlights of the specialty espresso calendar. For food pairings, Tarrazu's honey sweetness and citrus brightness pair beautifully with light pastries: madeleines, lemon poppy seed cake, shortbread, and fresh fruit. The clean finish also makes it an excellent companion to a simple breakfast of yogurt with honey and sliced stone fruit — mirroring the peach and apricot notes in the cup.

key_takeaways: Tarrazu is defined by honey sweetness, citrus brightness, and an impeccably clean finish — the most approachable specialty single-origin espresso.
Costa Rica's quality-first culture (Robusta ban, hand-picking, advanced processing) ensures remarkable consistency from crop to crop.
Brew at 93 C with a 1:2 to 1:2.3 ratio — forgiving and rewarding across the entire Arco machine and grinder range.
Exceptional both black and iced, where the clean finish and high sweetness translate beautifully to cold service.
Look for fresh-crop Tarrazu from March to June for peak sweetness and complexity.
